programs.name: Medicaid for Aged, Blind, Disabled (ABD)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+, blind, or disabled, they may qualify for comprehensive Medicaid health coverage and home care services.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65 or older, blind, or disabled, New Hampshire's Medicaid ABD program can cover their medical care and potentially pay for home-based services to help them stay independent. ABD is actually five different programs under one umbrella — from basic health coverage to comprehensive home care that includes help with bathing, dressing, and meals.

The financial requirements vary significantly by which ABD program your parent needs. For long-term care services, they can have up to $7,500 in assets (though the official limit is $2,500, New Hampshire applies a disregard). Your parent's home is protected if you or a disabled family member lives there — otherwise, home equity cannot exceed $752,000 (2026).

Because each ABD program has different income limits and benefits, most families need to call directly to determine which program fits their parent's situation. The state recommends applying even if you think your parent's income is too high — exceptions and disregards often apply.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if spouse or disabled child lives there)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For home care services, your parent must need help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or managing medications (Activities of Daily Living assessment).
otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through HealthCare.gov or call New Hampshire DHHS — processing time varies by program.

steps.title: Determine which ABD program fits
steps.description: Call 1-800-852-3345, ext. 9700 to identify whether your parent needs basic health coverage, working disabled coverage (MOAD), or home care services (Choices for Independence).

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect income statements, bank accounts, asset information, and medical records. Requirements differ by ABD program.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at HealthCare.gov, call 1-800-852-3345 ext. 9700, or visit a local DHHS office.

steps.title: Complete assessment if needed
steps.description: For home care services, your parent will need a functional assessment to determine care needs and service levels.

applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if you think your parent's income is too high — New Hampshire has income disregards and exceptions that aren't widely publicized.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500
excluded: Primary home (if spouse or disabled child lives there)

contentSections.text: If your parent qualifies for long-term care ABD services, they must contribute nearly all their monthly income toward care costs. They keep only $93/month for personal needs, plus Medicare premiums.
ABD covers five different programs with different rules. Don't assume your parent doesn't qualify — call 1-800-852-3345, ext. 9700 to find out which program matches their specific situation.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she gets ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you, your spouse, a child under 21, or a permanently disabled child lives in the home. Otherwise, her home equity cannot exceed $752,000 (2026). The home is automatically protected in the first scenario.

faqs.question: What if Dad is still working but disabled — can he get ABD?
faqs.answer: Yes, MOAD (Medicaid for Employed Older Adults with Disabilities) is specifically for working disabled adults 65+. It has higher income and asset limits than other ABD programs, though specific amounts aren't published.

faqs.question: How much of Mom's Social Security will she have to pay toward her care?
faqs.answer: If she gets long-term care services, she keeps only $93/month for personal needs, plus Medicare premiums. The rest of her income goes toward care costs. This is called Patient Liability.

faqs.question: What's the difference between regular Medicaid and ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: ABD Medicaid is for people 65+, blind, or disabled and often includes home care services like help with bathing and meals. Regular Medicaid is primarily for low-income adults and families without these specific conditions.

faqs.question: Can Dad have both Medicare and ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, many people have both. Medicaid ABD often helps pay Medicare premiums and covers services Medicare doesn't, like long-term home care and personal care assistance.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway. New Hampshire has income disregards and spend-down options that aren't well-publicized. Medical expenses can sometimes reduce countable income, and different ABD programs have different thresholds.

programs.name: Choices for Independence (CFI) Waiver
programs.tagline: Get nursing-home-level care coordination and support services so your parent can stay at home instead of entering a facility.

localEntities.name: Local DHHS offices, ADRC/ServiceLink centers, NH Care Collaborative, Community Partners

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay at home, New Hampshire's CFI Waiver can provide the services to make that possible. The program covers care coordination, case management, and home-based supports — essentially bringing nursing home services to your parent's house.

To qualify, your parent must be medically eligible for nursing home placement (meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management) and meet Medicaid's financial guidelines. Because this uses Medicaid funding, your parent will need to meet stricter income and asset limits than regular Medicaid — specifically the nursing home level of care thresholds.

Services are determined through an individual assessment and coordinated by local case managers. What your parent receives depends on their specific needs and what's available in your area.

summary: Nursing home level of care needed
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be medically eligible for nursing home placement (needs daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management)
otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
In need of services to live at home

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at NHEasy.NH.gov or call ServiceLink at 1-866-634-9412 — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact ServiceLink
steps.description: Call 1-866-634-9412 or visit www.servicelink.nh.gov to connect with your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC)

steps.title: Complete Medicaid application
steps.description: Apply online at www.NHEasy.NH.gov or through your local DHHS office — you'll need to meet Medicaid financial guidelines for long-term care

steps.title: Get medical assessment
steps.description: DHHS will evaluate whether your parent meets nursing home level of care requirements through clinical assessment

steps.title: Assignment to case manager
steps.description: If approved, your parent will be assigned to a local case management agency (you can request a specific one)

applicationGuide.tip: Download the CFI program booklet from NHEasy.NH.gov before applying to understand what services might be available in your area.

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing need for daily assistance

contentSections.text: The financial eligibility uses Medicaid long-term care guidelines, which are stricter than regular Medicaid. Confirm current income and asset limits with DHHS before applying — your parent might qualify even if they don't qualify for other Medicaid programs.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: New Hampshire uses special Medicaid rules for long-term care that may allow higher income levels than regular Medicaid. Contact DHHS at 603-271-9700 to check current limits — they can also explain spend-down options if your parent's income is slightly too high.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is typically exempt from Medicaid asset limits. However, there may be equity caps and estate recovery rules after your parent passes. Discuss this with the DHHS caseworker during application.

faqs.question: What services will my parent actually get?
faqs.answer: Services are determined by individual assessment and vary by location. Common services include care coordination, case management, personal care assistance, and home modifications. Your case manager will work with you to identify what's available in your area.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent needs 'nursing home level of care'?
faqs.answer: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, transferring from bed to chair, using the bathroom, or managing medications, they likely qualify. DHHS will do a clinical assessment to make the official determination.

faqs.question: What happens if we're not eligible for CFI?
faqs.answer: The ADRC counselors will help you find alternatives. They know about other state programs, local resources, and private options that might work for your parent's situation.

faqs.question: Can we choose which case management agency to work with?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can request a specific case management agency when your parent is approved. The agencies vary by region, so your options depend on where your parent lives.

programs.name: NH PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All-inclusive care with one team handling everything — but not yet available in New Hampshire.
programs.intro: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay at home or in assisted living, PACE provides everything under one roof — primary care, specialists, medications, home care, transportation, meals, and adult day services. Because one interdisciplinary team manages all care, your parent won't juggle multiple providers or face deductibles and copays for PACE services. However, New Hampshire does not currently have any operational PACE programs. The state has discussed bringing PACE to New Hampshire, but no centers exist yet and families cannot enroll today.

summary: Able to live safely in community
Live near PACE center (none in NH)
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility — the level of care provided in nursing homes)
otherRequirements: Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Must live within service area of approved PACE provider (currently none in New Hampshire)

applicationGuide.summary: Cannot apply in New Hampshire — no PACE programs are operational in the state.

steps.title: Check for NH updates
steps.description: Monitor NH DHHS website for announcements about PACE program launch

steps.title: Consider neighboring states
steps.description: If your parent lives near state borders, check if nearby PACE centers in other states serve your area

steps.title: Get pre-qualified
steps.description: If PACE comes to NH, you'll need Nursing Facility Level of Care certification from the state

applicationGuide.tip: Keep medical records organized now. When PACE launches in NH, you'll need comprehensive documentation of your parent's care needs for the nursing facility level assessment.

categories.name: Medical Documentation
items: Complete medical records showing need for nursing home-level care
Documentation from doctors about daily care needs

categories.name: Personal Information
items: Proof of age (birth certificate or driver's license)
Proof of residence in PACE service area
Medicare card and Medicaid documentation if applicable

contentSections.text: New Hampshire has no operational PACE programs. Do not wait for PACE if your parent needs care now — explore other options like home health agencies, adult day programs, and assisted living facilities.
Once enrolled in PACE, your parent must use PACE providers for all care except emergencies. Out-of-network care may not be covered, so this is a 'lock-in' decision.

faqs.question: When will PACE be available in New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: No timeline has been announced. New Hampshire has discussed bringing PACE to the state, but no providers have been approved and no centers are under construction.

faqs.question: Can we use PACE in another state if we live near the border?
faqs.answer: Possibly. PACE centers typically serve areas within 30-45 minutes drive time. If you live near Massachusetts, Vermont, or Maine, check if their PACE centers serve your specific area.

faqs.question: What does 'nursing facility level of care' actually mean?
faqs.answer: Your parent needs significant daily help with basic activities — bathing, dressing, eating, medication management, or mobility. A state assessor evaluates whether your parent would qualify for nursing home admission based on these care needs.

faqs.question: How much would PACE cost if it comes to New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: Free for Medicaid-eligible participants. Medicare beneficiaries who don't qualify for Medicaid pay a monthly premium, but the exact amount varies by provider and isn't set for New Hampshire since no programs exist yet.

faqs.question: What if Mom needs care now and can't wait for PACE?
faqs.answer: Look into home health agencies covered by Medicare, adult day programs, Area Agency on Aging services, or assisted living facilities. These provide similar services but aren't as comprehensive as PACE.

programs.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualified Individual (QI/SLMB-135)
programs.tagline: Your parent's Medicare premiums and deductibles could be covered if their income is under $1,660/month.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with Medicare costs, New Hampshire's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate their monthly Medicare premium ($202.90) and all deductibles and copays. Because these programs work in tiers, your parent may qualify even if their income seems too high for other assistance — the highest tier covers people earning up to $1,660/month as a single person or $2,239/month as a couple.

The program automatically qualifies your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, which can save hundreds more each year. Because New Hampshire disregards the first $13 of unearned income (like Social Security), your parent may qualify even if their income appears to exceed the limits by a small amount.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums, deductibles, and copays based on program tier

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Parts A and B (even if not currently enrolled)
New Hampshire resident

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through New Hampshire DHHS Medicaid office by phone, mail, or in person.

steps.title: Contact NH DHHS
steps.description: Call New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services to request a Medicaid application form.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security award letters, pension statements, bank statements, and proof of Medicare enrollment.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the Medicaid application and submit with required documents by mail, in person, or online if available.

steps.title: Wait for determination
steps.description: DHHS will review the application and notify you of the decision and which tier your parent qualifies for.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI tier may have limited funding and operate on a first-come, first-served basis
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year for QI tier due to potential funding limits

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.description: Full coverage for all Medicare costs
tiers.coverage: Part A premiums (up to $565/month), Part B premiums ($202.90/month), all deductibles ($1,736 Part A, $283 Part B), all copays and coinsurance

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.description: Medicare Part B premium coverage only
tiers.coverage: Part B premiums ($202.90/month) plus automatic Extra Help for prescriptions

tiers.name: QI (Qualified Individual)
tiers.description: Medicare Part B premium coverage with limited funding
tiers.coverage: Part B premiums ($202.90/month) plus automatic Extra Help for prescriptions

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary residence

categories.name: Medicare
items: Proof of Medicare enrollment or eligibility

contentSections.text: QI tier has limited federal funding and may close to new applicants once the yearly allocation is exhausted. Apply early in the calendar year if your parent's income falls in this range.
New Hampshire automatically disregards the first $13 of unearned income like Social Security. If your parent's income appears slightly over the limit, they may still qualify.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary residence does not count toward the asset limits, regardless of its value. Your parent can own their home and still qualify for all tiers of Medicare Savings Programs.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the limit by $20-30?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify. New Hampshire disregards the first $13 of unearned income like Social Security or pensions when calculating eligibility. Have her apply even if her gross income appears slightly over the limits.

faqs.question: Will providers try to bill my parent for Medicare costs if they have QMB?
faqs.answer: They're not supposed to, but it happens. Providers cannot legally bill QMB recipients for Medicare deductibles, copays, or coinsurance. If your parent receives bills for these costs, contact the provider immediately to correct the error.

faqs.question: Does this affect my parent's regular Medicaid or other benefits?
faqs.answer: No, Medicare Savings Programs are separate from regular Medicaid and won't affect other benefits. These programs specifically help with Medicare costs and automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs.

faqs.question: How much will the Extra Help prescription benefit save?
faqs.answer: The automatic Extra Help qualification typically reduces prescription copays to $1.55 for generics and $4.60 for brand names in 2026, plus eliminates the Medicare Part D coverage gap. This can save hundreds to thousands per year depending on medication costs.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad's income changes after he's approved?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to NH DHHS. If his income increases above his current tier but stays within the overall program limits, he may move to a different tier with different benefits. If income drops, he may qualify for a higher tier with more coverage.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for parents on fixed incomes — up to $415/month for couples

localEntities.name: Local DHHS District Offices across NH.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP provides monthly money loaded onto an EBT card for food purchases. Because New Hampshire expanded eligibility beyond federal minimums, your parent may qualify even if their income seems too high — the state allows up to $2,608/month for a single person ($3,526 for couples). Most seniors who qualify receive between $100-415/month depending on household size and income.

Your parent can use SNAP benefits at any grocery store, farmers market, or retailer that accepts EBT cards. The money covers groceries like meat, dairy, produce, and pantry staples, but cannot be used for hot prepared foods, alcohol, or tobacco. Because SNAP has no asset limits for most New Hampshire households, your parent won't need to spend down savings to qualify.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly benefit amounts range $100-415 based on household size and income

otherRequirements: Must be U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Must live in New Hampshire

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at NHEasy — takes about 30 minutes and you'll get an answer within 30 days (7 days if urgent).

steps.title: Gather documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, bank statements, rent/mortgage receipts, and medical bills over $35/month

steps.title: Apply online at NHEasy
steps.description: Go to nheasy.nh.gov and complete the SNAP application — you can save and return if needed

steps.title: Submit proof documents
steps.description: Upload or mail copies of income, expense, and identity documents within 10 days

steps.title: Complete phone interview
steps.description: DHHS will call to verify information and answer questions about the application

steps.title: Get EBT card
steps.description: If approved, the EBT card arrives by mail within 7-10 days with benefits loaded

applicationGuide.processingTime: Generally 30 days; expedited for urgent cases within 7 days
applicationGuide.tip: Even if your parent's income seems too high, apply anyway — New Hampshire has higher limits than most states and medical expenses can reduce countable income.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2026)
contentSections.footnote: October 2025 - September 2026 limits for households with elderly (60+) members

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Only if income over $2,608)

contentSections.text: Medical expenses over $35/month can significantly boost eligibility for seniors. Include prescription costs, Medicare premiums, dental bills, and medical equipment — these reduce countable income dollar-for-dollar.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security puts them just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through the net income test. Medical expenses over $35/month, shelter costs, and other deductions can bring their countable income below the limit. New Hampshire also has higher income limits than most states — $2,608/month for a single person.

faqs.question: Will applying for SNAP affect my parent's other benefits?
faqs.answer: No. SNAP won't reduce Social Security, Medicare, or most other benefits. If your parent gets SSI, they may automatically qualify for SNAP through categorical eligibility.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to spend down their savings to qualify?
faqs.answer: Most likely not. New Hampshire eliminated asset limits for most households. Only if your parent's income is over $2,608/month ($3,526 for couples) would the $4,500 asset limit apply, and retirement accounts don't count anyway.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits at farmers markets?
faqs.answer: Yes. SNAP EBT cards work at most farmers markets in New Hampshire, and many offer matching programs that double the value of SNAP dollars spent on fresh produce.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after approval?
faqs.answer: Report changes within 10 days. If income goes up, benefits may decrease. If income drops (like losing a part-time job), benefits may increase. Changes are usually effective the month after reporting.

faqs.question: How long do SNAP benefits last?
faqs.answer: Benefits are ongoing but require recertification every 12-24 months depending on your parent's situation. Elderly households typically recertify every 24 months with simpler paperwork.

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Your parent could get $94–$2,177 toward heating bills if their income is under $3,967/month.

localEntities.name: Belknap/Merrimack (Concord: 603-223-0043)
Rockingham (Portsmouth: 603-436-3896)
Strafford (Dover: 603-435-2500)
Other regions via local Community Action Partnership of NH (CAPNH)

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with heating bills during New Hampshire winters, LIHEAP provides direct payments to their heating company — not cash they manage. Because benefits range from $94 to $2,177 per heating season based on income and household size, most families see meaningful bill relief.

The program prioritizes households with elderly adults, disabled family members, or children under 6. Because LIHEAP pays vendors directly, your parent never handles the money, and these payments don't count as income for other benefit programs like SNAP or Medicaid.

Applications open around July 30 each year, but funding runs out before winter ends. Because priority households can pre-apply starting July 1, elderly parents should apply as early as possible to secure assistance.
programs.savingsSource: Heating assistance range paid directly to heating vendor

otherRequirements: Must reside in New Hampshire
Available for heating season only (fall/winter)
Household includes everyone at address sharing utility bills, including roommates

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Community Action Agency for a phone interview appointment — applications start around July 30, 2025.

steps.title: Find your regional office
steps.description: Contact the Community Action Agency serving your parent's county by phone to schedule an intake interview.

steps.title: Submit pre-application (if priority)
steps.description: If your parent is elderly, disabled, or has children under 6 in the household, they can pre-apply starting July 1.

steps.title: Complete intake interview
steps.description: Agency staff will conduct a phone or in-person interview and provide a document checklist specific to your parent's situation.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Provide income proof, heating bills, and household information as specified during the interview.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; apply early as funds are limited
applicationGuide.tip: Apply as early as possible when applications open — funding runs out before the heating season ends.

categories.name: Financial
items: Social Security or pension benefit statements
Recent heating bills showing fuel type

contentSections.text: Applications open around July 30, 2025, and funding runs out before winter ends. Priority households (elderly, disabled, families with young children) can pre-apply starting July 1.
Everyone living at the same address counts as one household if they share utility bills — even non-related roommates. This affects both income calculations and benefit amounts.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the monthly limit?
faqs.answer: If their gross monthly income exceeds the LIHEAP limits but falls within 75% of state median income (like $4,059/month for one person), they may qualify for related emergency heating assistance programs administered by the same agencies.

faqs.question: Can my parent get help if their heat gets shut off?
faqs.answer: Yes. Winter crisis assistance up to $2,177 is available for emergencies like utility shutoffs or broken heating equipment, separate from regular heating assistance.

faqs.question: Does this help with electric bills for air conditioning?
faqs.answer: No. New Hampshire LIHEAP only covers heating costs during fall and winter months. No cooling assistance is available for summer electric bills.

faqs.question: Will getting LIHEAP affect my parent's other benefits?
faqs.answer: No. LIHEAP payments go directly to heating vendors and don't count as income for SNAP, Medicaid, or other benefit programs.

faqs.question: What if my parent rents and doesn't pay the heating bill directly?
faqs.answer: Renters are eligible if they pay heating costs separately or if heat is included in rent. The agency will work with landlords or tenants depending on the billing arrangement.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive?
faqs.answer: Benefits range from $94 to $2,177 based on household income, size, fuel type, and priority status. Elderly households and those with lower incomes typically receive higher amounts.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements can reduce your parent's heating bills by making their home warmer and more efficient.

localEntities.name: CAPBM: Merrimack/Belknap Counties (capbm.org)
CAPHR: Hillsborough/Rockingham Counties (caphr.org)
CAPSC: Strafford County (straffordcap.org, 603-435-2500 x 2350)

programs.intro:
If your parent's home is drafty, hard to heat, or driving up energy bills, New Hampshire's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free energy improvements. The program includes a professional energy audit, air sealing, insulation, LED bulbs, low-flow water devices, and sometimes refrigerator replacement or heating system repairs.

Your parent must first qualify for New Hampshire's Fuel Assistance Program or Electric Assistance Program (typically households at or below 200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines). Priority goes to elderly households, people with disabilities, families with children under 6, and those with high energy costs. Because the program focuses on energy conservation, homes with major structural problems like leaking roofs or failing electrical systems don't qualify.

The program is administered regionally by Community Action Partnership agencies, so where your parent lives determines which office handles their application. Renters can participate, but need landlord permission for the work.

summary: Must qualify for Fuel or Electric Assistance
Income typically at or below 200% Federal Poverty Guidelines
otherRequirements: Must qualify for New Hampshire Fuel Assistance Program (FAP) or Electric Assistance Program (EAP)
Home cannot have major structural issues, leaking roof, or failing plumbing/electrical systems
Homes weatherized after September 1994 generally ineligible
Renters need landlord permission
Multi-unit buildings need 66% of units enrolled in fuel/electric assistance

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through the Fuel Assistance or Electric Assistance Program application and answer 'YES' to the weatherization question.

steps.title: Apply for Fuel or Electric Assistance
steps.description: Complete the FAP or EAP application through your regional Community Action Partnership office. This is required before weatherization services.

steps.title: Request weatherization services
steps.description: Answer 'YES' to the weatherization interest question on your fuel assistance application, or contact your regional CAP office directly.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: If eligible, a professional will conduct a free energy audit of your parent's home to determine what improvements are needed.

steps.title: Approve recommended work
steps.description: Review the audit recommendations with the CAP agency. Work is prioritized based on cost-effectiveness and health/safety standards.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified - involves energy audit, contractor work, and follow-up inspection
applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding is limited and priority tiers affect access
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the heating season as demand is highest during winter months.

offices.name: CAPSC
offices.address: 577 Central Ave. Suite 10, Dover NH 03820 - Serves Strafford County

contentSections.heading: What to Bring for Fuel Assistance Application

categories.name: Housing
items: Landlord contact information and permission (if renter)

contentSections.text: Homes weatherized after September 1994 are generally ineligible for the program. Check with your local CAP office if you're unsure about previous work.
Renters can participate, but you must get landlord permission before any work begins. The improvements benefit both tenant and property owner.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the fuel assistance limit?
faqs.answer: Unfortunately, weatherization assistance requires qualifying for New Hampshire's Fuel Assistance Program or Electric Assistance Program first. There's no separate income limit for weatherization - you must be eligible for the underlying energy assistance programs.

faqs.question: Can my parent get weatherization help if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the landlord must give written permission for the work. For multi-unit buildings, at least 66% of the units must be enrolled in fuel or electric assistance programs.

faqs.question: What if their home was weatherized years ago - can they get help again?
faqs.answer: Homes weatherized after September 1994 are generally not eligible for additional weatherization services. However, each case is reviewed individually, so contact your regional Community Action Partnership office to discuss your parent's specific situation.

faqs.question: Will the program fix my parent's broken furnace?
faqs.answer: The program focuses on energy conservation, not major repairs. Limited heating system repairs or replacements may be included if they're cost-effective and meet health/safety standards. However, homes with major structural issues, failing electrical systems, or leaking roofs don't qualify.

faqs.question: How long does the weatherization process take?
faqs.answer: The timeline isn't specified, but involves multiple steps: qualifying for fuel assistance, scheduling an energy audit, contractor work, and a follow-up inspection. Because funding is limited and there are priority tiers, the wait time can vary significantly.

faqs.question: Who gets priority for weatherization services?
faqs.answer: Priority goes to elderly households, people with disabilities, families with children under 6, households with high energy costs, and long-term Fuel Assistance Program participants. Your parent's priority level affects how quickly they receive services.

programs.name: SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Free expert help to navigate Medicare options and avoid overpaying for coverage.

localEntities.name: Administered statewide by ServiceLink Resource Center (NH DHHS Bureau of Elderly & Adult Services in Concord); local access via phone/website statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare and you're confused about their coverage options, SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help you understand benefits, compare plans, and avoid overpaying. Because Medicare has dozens of plan options that change annually, having an expert walk through your parent's specific situation can save hundreds of dollars per year in premiums and out-of-pocket costs.

SHIP counselors help with Medicare Part A/B, Part D prescription coverage, Medigap supplements, Medicare Advantage plans, and Medicare Savings Programs. They also explain long-term care insurance options and can refer you to prescription assistance programs. No income limits apply — this free service is available to all Medicare beneficiaries and their families in New Hampshire.

otherRequirements: Must have questions about Medicare or health insurance coverage

applicationGuide.summary: Call 866-634-9412 for immediate counseling during business hours or to schedule an appointment.

steps.title: Call the SHIP hotline
steps.description: Call 866-634-9412 between 8am-4pm. No forms or documents needed for initial consultation.

steps.title: Describe your parent's situation
steps.description: Explain what Medicare questions you have — comparing plans, understanding costs, or enrollment deadlines.

steps.title: Schedule follow-up if needed
steps.description: For complex situations, schedule a longer appointment. Counselor may ask you to gather Medicare card or current plan details.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate counseling available by phone or appointment
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's Medicare number ready, but counselors can help even without it for general questions.

offices.name: ServiceLink Resource Center
offices.address: NH DHHS Bureau of Elderly & Adult Services, 105 Pleasant St., Gov. Gallan State Office Park S., Concord NH 03301-3857

contentSections.text: SHIP counselors provide information and guidance but cannot enroll your parent in plans directly. They'll explain your options and help you complete enrollment through the appropriate channels.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent already has Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors help people review existing coverage annually, compare new plan options, and identify potential savings. Because Medicare plans change their costs and coverage each year, an annual review can prevent overpaying.

faqs.question: Will they try to sell us insurance?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP counselors are volunteers who don't sell insurance or receive commissions. Their only goal is helping you understand options objectively. All counseling is confidential and no personal information is shared unless you request it.

faqs.question: Can they help with Medicare Savings Programs that reduce premiums?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors explain Medicare Savings Programs like QMB and SLMB that can eliminate Medicare premiums for people with income up to $1,660/month (single) or $2,239/month (couple). They'll help you understand if your parent qualifies and where to apply.

faqs.question: Do we need an appointment or can we call anytime?
faqs.answer: You can call 866-634-9412 between 8am-4pm for immediate help with simple questions. For complex situations requiring detailed plan comparisons, they'll schedule a longer appointment at a convenient time.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help with prescription drug costs?
faqs.answer: Yes. They help compare Medicare Part D plans based on your parent's specific medications, explain the coverage gap (donut hole), and provide information about prescription assistance programs that can reduce out-of-pocket costs.

faqs.question: What if we live in a rural area of New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: SHIP services are available statewide by phone and website. You don't need to travel to Concord — all counseling can be done over the phone with the same personalized attention as in-person meetings.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Elderly Nutrition Program)
programs.tagline: Free home-delivered meals for your parent if they're homebound and struggling to shop or cook.

localEntities.name: Rockingham Nutrition & Meals on Wheels (Rockingham County)
Meals on Wheels of Hillsborough County
Gibson Center (specific NH towns: Albany, Bartlett, etc.)
Coalition via Meals on Wheels NH (https://mealsonwheelsnh.org)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and homebound — whether due to mobility issues, lack of transportation, or difficulty shopping and cooking — Meals on Wheels delivers nutritionally balanced meals directly to their door at no cost. The program provides typically 5 meals per week (one per weekday), designed by dietitians to meet older adults' nutritional needs.

Because Meals on Wheels operates through local nonprofits rather than as a statewide program, your parent must live within a specific provider's delivery zone to qualify. Each provider covers certain counties or towns, so location determines both eligibility and how to apply.

Unlike many programs, there are no income limits for adults 60 and older — the primary requirement is being homebound or having difficulty meeting basic nutritional needs. However, there may be waitlists depending on demand in your area.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or have difficulty leaving home, shopping, and preparing meals — whether due to mobility, transportation, or health issues
otherRequirements: Must live within the delivery zone of a local Meals on Wheels provider
Unable to meet basic nutritional needs independently

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local provider directly — some accept online referrals while others require phone calls, with service typically starting within a week if no waitlist.

steps.title: Find your local provider
steps.description: Use the Meals on Wheels NH website at mealsonwheelsnh.org to locate which provider serves your parent's town or county.

steps.title: Contact the provider directly
steps.description: Call or submit an online referral form. They'll assess your parent's needs and delivery area eligibility.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment
steps.description: Provide basic information about your parent's age, address, dietary restrictions, and why they need meal delivery.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Most providers need proof of age and residence, plus emergency contact information. Financial documents only required if your parent is under 60.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by provider; some within a week, longer if waitlist exists
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible depending on program capacity and local demand
applicationGuide.tip: Start with your local Area Agency on Aging rather than national websites — they handle the actual applications and know current availability.

offices.name: Meals on Wheels of Hillsborough County
Gibson Center Meals on Wheels
offices.address: Serves Albany, Bartlett, and surrounding towns

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate)
Proof of residence in the provider's service area

categories.name: Medical & Dietary
items: List of dietary restrictions or special needs

categories.name: Financial (Under 60 Only)
items: Income documentation if your parent is under 60 with a disability
Proof of disability status for adults under 60

contentSections.text: Service is not statewide. Your parent must live within a specific provider's delivery zone. If they live outside these areas, they cannot receive meals through this program.
If your parent owns a car and can easily leave home for shopping, they may not qualify. The program prioritizes truly homebound seniors who cannot meet their nutritional needs independently.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives just outside the delivery area?
faqs.answer: They cannot receive Meals on Wheels service. The program operates through local nonprofits with specific geographic boundaries. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to explore other nutrition programs that might serve their area.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose what meals they receive?
faqs.answer: Most providers accommodate dietary restrictions and medical needs (diabetic, low-sodium, texture-modified meals), but daily menu choices are typically limited. Meals are planned by dietitians to meet nutritional requirements for older adults.

faqs.question: What if my parent can still drive but has trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify if they have difficulty shopping or preparing nutritionally adequate meals, even with transportation. The assessment focuses on their ability to maintain proper nutrition, not just mobility.

faqs.question: How long can my parent receive meals?
faqs.answer: There's no time limit as long as they continue to meet eligibility requirements and need the service. The program serves both temporary needs (recovering from surgery) and long-term situations.

faqs.question: What if there's a waitlist in our area?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies by provider and local demand. Some programs may prioritize based on medical need or lack of other support. Contact the provider to understand current wait times and your parent's priority level.

faqs.question: Can my parent's spouse also receive meals?
faqs.answer: Possibly, but verify with your local provider. Some programs serve spouses even if they don't meet all eligibility criteria individually, while others require separate qualification.

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.tagline: Get respite care, counseling, and support services when caring for an aging parent or disabled family member — no income limits.

localEntities.name: Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)
Granite State Independent Living

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging parent 60+ or a family member with Alzheimer's, New Hampshire's Family Caregiver Support Program provides respite care (temporary breaks), counseling, and practical help like home modifications and transportation. The program serves adult children helping parents, grandparents raising grandchildren, and anyone caring for a family member with disabilities.

Because there are no income limits, you can qualify regardless of your family's financial situation. Services include individual counseling, support groups, caregiver training, and limited assistance with chore services and assistive equipment. Different caregiver types have different age requirements — for example, grandparents caring for children under 18 must be 55+ themselves.

summary: Caring for family member 60+ or with disabilities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Care recipient must need help with at least two Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or continence)
otherRequirements: Must be family member or informal caregiver
Specific age requirements vary by caregiver type
For dementia care: must live under same roof
For grandparent caregivers: child must live full-time with caregiver

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Aging and Disability Resource Center — they'll assess your needs and connect you with services.

steps.title: Call the statewide number
steps.description: Call 866-634-9412 to be connected to your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC)

steps.title: Describe your caregiving situation
steps.description: Explain who you're caring for, their needs (especially help with daily activities like bathing or dressing), and what type of support you need

steps.title: Schedule an assessment
steps.description: Your ADRC will assess your caregiver needs and your family member's care requirements to determine which services you qualify for

steps.title: Get connected to services
steps.description: Based on your assessment, you'll be connected to respite care, counseling, support groups, or other services in your area

applicationGuide.tip: Prepare details about your family member's daily care needs — specifically which activities they need help with (bathing, dressing, medication management, etc.)

tiers.name: Adult Children Caring for Parents
tiers.description: Adult family members (18+) caring for someone 60+
tiers.coverage: Respite care, counseling, support groups, caregiver training, limited home modifications

tiers.name: Dementia/Alzheimer's Caregivers
tiers.description: Caregivers (18+) for family members with Alzheimer's or related disorders
tiers.coverage: Must live under same roof. Care recipient unable to follow through after cuing.

tiers.name: Grandparent Caregivers
tiers.description: Grandparents or relatives (55+) caring for children under 18
tiers.coverage: Child must live full-time with caregiver in absence of parent

tiers.name: Disability Caregivers
tiers.coverage: Priority given to those caring for adults with severe disabilities

categories.name: Care Recipient Information
items: Details about daily activities they need help with (bathing, dressing, medication management)
Living situation and relationship to you

categories.name: Caregiver Details
items: Your age and relationship to care recipient
Type of support you need most (respite care, counseling, equipment, etc.)
Current caregiving responsibilities and time commitment

contentSections.text: This program provides support services, not direct payments to caregivers. If you need paid caregiver services, ask about Medicaid programs like Consumer Directed Care.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through this program?
faqs.answer: No, this program doesn't provide direct payments to caregivers. It offers support services like respite care, counseling, and training. For paid caregiving programs, ask about Medicaid options like Consumer Directed Care when you call.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in their own home but I don't live with them?
faqs.answer: You can still qualify as long as you're providing regular care to a parent 60+ who needs help with daily activities. The 'same roof' requirement only applies to caregivers of people with dementia.

faqs.question: How much respite care can I get?
faqs.answer: The program doesn't specify exact hours, but respite care is designed to give full-time caregivers temporary breaks. Your local ADRC will assess your needs and available funding to determine what's available in your area.

faqs.question: What if my income is too high for other programs?
faqs.answer: This program has no income limits, so higher-income families can still access support services. This makes it different from Medicaid programs that have strict financial requirements.

faqs.question: Can I get help with home modifications for my parent's safety?
faqs.answer: Yes, the program offers 'limited supplemental services' including home modifications and assistive equipment. However, these are limited by funding, so priority may go to those with the greatest need.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and other caregiver programs?
faqs.answer: This program focuses on support services (counseling, respite, training) rather than direct care or payment. It's funded through the Older Americans Act and has no income requirements, making it more accessible than Medicaid programs.

programs.name: SCSEP (Senior Community Service Employment Program)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid work training for unemployed adults 55+ to build skills and transition to permanent employment.

localEntities.name: Operation ABLE (primary NH SCSEP provider spotlighted for NH service areas)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and unemployed, SCSEP provides part-time paid work (average 20 hours per week) at community organizations like schools, hospitals, and senior centers while they build job skills. Participants earn at least minimum wage while receiving job search assistance to move into permanent employment.

This is a temporary training program — not permanent employment — designed to help older adults refresh their skills and find unsubsidized jobs. Your parent must be unemployed when they apply and have family income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level.
programs.savingsSource: Paid training position with job placement assistance

otherRequirements: Currently unemployed
New Hampshire resident
Eligible to work in the United States
Seeking to learn or refresh job skills

applicationGuide.summary: Call Operation ABLE or the national SCSEP line to start enrollment — processing time varies by demand and priority status.

steps.title: Contact Operation ABLE
steps.description: Call Operation ABLE (New Hampshire's SCSEP provider) or the national line at 1-877-872-5627 to discuss eligibility and available positions in your area.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of age, income, unemployment status, NH residency, and work authorization before your appointment.

steps.title: Complete enrollment
steps.description: Meet with program staff to verify eligibility, discuss job skills and interests, and get matched with available community service positions.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Enrollment priority given to veterans, adults over 65, and those with disabilities or other barriers — non-priority applicants may wait longer
applicationGuide.tip: Veterans and adults over 65 get priority enrollment, so mention veteran status when calling if applicable.

categories.name: Identity & Eligibility
items: Proof of age (birth certificate or driver's license)
Proof of NH residency (utility bill or lease)
Work authorization documents (Social Security card, driver's license)

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of family income for all household members

contentSections.text: SCSEP has enrollment priorities. Veterans and their spouses get first priority, followed by adults over 65, people with disabilities, and those with limited English or low literacy. If your parent doesn't fall into priority categories, they may need to wait for an opening.

This is temporary paid training, not permanent employment. The goal is to build skills and connections that lead to unsubsidized jobs. Most people participate for several months to a year before transitioning to regular employment.

faqs.question: How much will my parent earn in the program?
faqs.answer: Participants earn at least minimum wage (whichever is higher: federal, state, or local minimum wage) for an average of 20 hours per week of community service work.

faqs.question: What kind of work will my parent do?
faqs.answer: Community service positions are at non-profit organizations and public agencies like schools, hospitals, day-care centers, and senior centers. The specific role depends on your parent's skills, interests, and what's available in your area.

faqs.question: How long can my parent stay in the program?
faqs.answer: SCSEP is designed as a temporary bridge to permanent employment. The exact time limit varies, but the goal is to help participants find unsubsidized jobs within several months to a year.

faqs.question: What counts as family income for the 125% poverty level limit?
faqs.answer: Income from all household members counts, and the exact dollar limits change annually based on household size. Contact Operation ABLE for current income limits — they're updated each January.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they've been retired but now need to work?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as they're currently unemployed and meet the other requirements. Previous retirement doesn't disqualify someone if they're now actively seeking employment.

faqs.question: Will this affect my parent's other benefits like Social Security?
faqs.answer: SCSEP earnings count as income and may affect need-based benefits. Contact Operation ABLE to discuss how participation might impact your parent's specific benefit situation.

programs.name: Legal Assistance for Older Adults
programs.tagline: Free legal help for New Hampshire seniors facing housing disputes, benefit appeals, or estate planning needs.

localEntities.name: Primary office: Concord, NH; statewide centralized intake via 603 Legal Aid/NHLA

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and facing legal issues like landlord disputes, Medicare denials, or needs help with wills, New Hampshire's Legal Assistance for Older Adults provides free civil legal services. Because this program covers everything from housing problems to estate planning, your parent gets specialized help without paying attorney fees that could cost thousands.

The program serves all New Hampshire residents 60+ regardless of income, though they prioritize low-income seniors. Services include court representation, negotiating with landlords, appealing government benefit denials, and creating essential documents like power of attorney. One centralized intake system handles applications statewide, so you don't need to find the right local office.

otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
Must have civil legal issues relevant to seniors (housing, benefits, family law, consumer issues, estate planning)

applicationGuide.summary: Call the centralized intake line at 1-800-639-5290 for screening and case assignment.

steps.title: Call centralized intake
steps.description: Call 1-800-639-5290 (603 Legal Aid) or 1-888-353-9944 (Senior Law Project) to describe your parent's legal issue

steps.title: Complete intake screening
steps.description: Staff will assess the case type and your parent's eligibility during the phone call

steps.title: Get assigned to attorney
steps.description: If accepted, you'll be connected with a lawyer who specializes in senior legal issues

applicationGuide.tip: Have details ready about the specific legal problem — dates, documents, and what outcome your parent needs.

offices.name: NH Legal Assistance (Main Office)
offices.address: 93 North State Street, Suite 200, Concord, NH 03301 (intake 9:00 am - 12:30 pm Mon-Wed)

contentSections.text: While there are no income limits, the program prioritizes low-income and isolated seniors. If your parent has significant resources, they may be referred to private attorneys instead.

faqs.question: What types of legal problems can this program help with?
faqs.answer: The program handles civil legal issues common to seniors: landlord-tenant disputes, utility shutoffs, Medicare/Medicaid appeals, debt collection, consumer fraud, family law issues, estate planning, wills, power of attorney, and probate matters. They cannot help with criminal cases.

faqs.question: Will my parent get a lawyer or just advice?
faqs.answer: Both. The program provides full legal representation in court and at hearings, plus legal advice, document preparation, and negotiation with landlords or creditors. Your parent gets the same services as hiring a private attorney.

faqs.question: My parent has some savings — can they still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. There are no asset or income limits, though the program prioritizes seniors with limited resources. If your parent has significant assets, they may be encouraged to hire a private attorney instead.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get help once we apply?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified, but urgent issues like eviction notices or benefit denials typically get faster response. The intake screening happens immediately when you call.

faqs.question: Can this program help if my parent lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program specifically serves institutionalized older adults and can help with facility-related legal issues, Medicaid planning, or any other civil legal problems your parent faces.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs help with something that isn't covered?
faqs.answer: The intake staff will let you know if the issue falls outside their scope and can often refer you to other resources or private attorneys who specialize in that area of law.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your parent faces problems in nursing homes or assisted living facilities.

localEntities.name: Central office: 129 Pleasant Street, Concord, NH 03301-3857

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a New Hampshire nursing home, assisted living facility, or other long-term care setting and faces problems with their care, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman provides free, confidential advocacy to resolve complaints and protect their rights.

The program helps with care quality issues, billing disputes, facility policy concerns, and ensures your parent is treated with dignity and respect. Because ombudsmen are independent advocates (not employed by facilities), they can investigate complaints without conflicts of interest and work directly with facility staff, families, and residents to find solutions.

This service is available to all facility residents in New Hampshire regardless of income, and families can request help on behalf of their loved ones. The program covers nursing homes, assisted living facilities, adult day care centers, long-term rehabilitation centers, and hospice centers statewide.

summary: Resident of NH long-term care facility
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in New Hampshire, including nursing homes, assisted living facilities, long-term rehabilitation centers, adult day care facilities, or hospice centers

applicationGuide.summary: Call the toll-free number to discuss issues — no forms or documents needed.

steps.title: Call the ombudsman office
steps.description: Phone (800) 442-5640 or (603) 271-4375 between 8:00 am - 4:30 pm to discuss your parent's situation.

steps.title: Explain the problem
steps.description: Describe the care issue, billing problem, or concern about your parent's treatment at their facility.

steps.title: Ombudsman investigates
steps.description: The ombudsman will work confidentially with the facility, your family, and your parent to resolve the complaint.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Services provided as needed upon contact
applicationGuide.tip: You can call on behalf of your parent even if they haven't asked you to — families and facility staff can request ombudsman services.

contentSections.text: This program only helps with facility-based care. If your parent receives care at home, the ombudsman cannot assist with home care provider issues.

faqs.question: Can I call the ombudsman if my parent doesn't want me to get involved?
faqs.answer: Yes. Family members can request ombudsman services even without the resident's permission, though the ombudsman will work to involve your parent in the process when possible.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my parent for filing a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal, and the ombudsman is specifically trained to prevent and address retaliation. They work confidentially and can take additional steps to protect your parent if retaliation occurs.

faqs.question: Will calling the ombudsman cost my parent their spot at the facility?
faqs.answer: No. Facilities cannot discharge residents for filing complaints or requesting ombudsman services. This would be illegal retaliation.

faqs.question: How long does it take to resolve complaints?
faqs.answer: Resolution time varies by issue complexity, but ombudsmen provide services as needed and work actively on cases once contacted. Simple issues may resolve quickly, while complex care problems may take longer.

faqs.question: Can the ombudsman help if my parent's Medicare claim was denied?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman focuses on facility-related issues like care quality and treatment. For Medicare billing disputes, contact Medicare directly at 1-800-MEDICARE or your State Health Insurance Assistance Program.

faqs.question: What's the difference between calling the ombudsman versus the state health department?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman provides advocacy and works to resolve issues through collaboration. The health department conducts formal investigations and can issue citations. The ombudsman may refer serious safety issues to the health department when needed.

programs.name: Adult Day Services
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily supervision and health monitoring, this Medicaid program provides medical day care for 4+ hours on scheduled days.

localEntities.name: Licensed under He-P 818; examples include facilities like Maple Leaf in southern NH, Rockingham County programs

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily supervision but isn't ready for nursing home care, Adult Day Services provides medical day care in a community setting. Your parent receives health monitoring, medication management, therapeutic activities, nutritious meals, and socialization for at least 4 hours per scheduled day.

This is a Medicaid-funded program for New Hampshire residents who are at risk of nursing home placement without these services. Because it's tied to Medicaid eligibility, your parent must meet strict income and asset limits: $2,901/month income and $2,500 in countable assets for a single person in 2025. The program requires a recent physician exam and referral, and excludes those whose primary need is mental health or developmental disability care.

summary: At risk of nursing home placement
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be at risk for immediate nursing home placement without services and need care at least 4 hours per day on a regularly scheduled basis
otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Living in community (not institution)
Physical exam and physician referral within 60 days
Diagnosed with subacute or chronic illness
Needs cannot exceed provider capacity

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through New Hampshire DHHS Medicaid intake, then get physician referral and provider assessment.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Contact New Hampshire DHHS Bureau of Elderly and Adult Services to start Medicaid eligibility screening

steps.title: Get physician exam
steps.description: Schedule physical exam and obtain physician referral with orders for adult day services (must be within 60 days)

steps.title: Find licensed provider
steps.description: Contact licensed adult day service providers in your area for assessment and enrollment

steps.title: Complete provider assessment
steps.description: Provider will assess eligibility under He-E 803.04 rules and develop care plan

applicationGuide.tip: Start with the Medicaid application first - you cannot access adult day services without Medicaid eligibility.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Burial funds up to certain limits

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status

categories.name: Medical
items: Physician referral with orders for adult day services
Medical records documenting chronic or subacute illness

contentSections.text: Your parent must have a physician exam and referral within 60 days of requesting services. The provider can deny services if your parent's care needs exceed what they can safely provide.
This program requires at least 4 hours per day commitment on scheduled days. It's designed for people who need daily supervision but can still live at home with support.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for this program?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as she intends to return to it. Only countable assets like bank accounts and investments count toward the $2,500 limit for single applicants.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,901 limit?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify for Medicaid, which means he can't access Adult Day Services through this program. You may need to explore private-pay adult day programs or other community resources.

faqs.question: Does this program provide transportation to the day center?
faqs.answer: Transportation isn't specified in the program requirements. Contact individual providers to ask about transportation services - some may offer it while others may require you to arrange transport.

faqs.question: Can Mom use this service just one day per week?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as she receives at least 4 hours of care on each scheduled day. The program allows for 1+ days per week on a regularly scheduled basis.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom's care needs increase beyond what the day program can handle?
faqs.answer: The provider will reassess her needs every 90 days. If her needs exceed what they can safely provide, they can discontinue services. At that point, you may need to explore nursing home care or other intensive services.

faqs.question: Are there different adult day programs to choose from in New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: Yes, there are licensed providers statewide, though availability varies by location. Examples include facilities like Maple Leaf in southern NH and Rockingham County programs. Each provider may offer different specific services within the program requirements.

programs.name: Easterseals NH In-Home Care & Health Services
programs.tagline: Professional nursing, personal care, and homemaker services in your parent's home when they need help but want to stay independent.

localEntities.name: Offices in Manchester and Keene for related services, but in-home care via statewide team

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with daily tasks like bathing, medication management, or light housekeeping but wants to stay in their own home, Easterseals NH provides professional in-home care services across New Hampshire. This isn't a government program — it's a nonprofit care provider that accepts Medicaid, long-term care insurance, Veterans' benefits, or private payment.

Services include nursing care (medication management, health monitoring), personal care (bathing, dressing assistance), and homemaker services (meal preparation, light cleaning, errands). Because Easterseals creates personalized care plans after a home visit, your parent gets exactly the support they need rather than a one-size-fits-all package.

Availability depends on staffing in your area. Easterseals focuses on the Seacoast, Greater Nashua, Greater Manchester, and Greater Rochester regions, with statewide coverage through their Caring Companions Program for non-medical care.

summary: Older adults or people with chronic illness/disabilities
Accepts Medicaid, insurance, Veterans' benefits, or private pay
Subject to staff availability in your area
otherRequirements: Must need nursing, homemaker, or personal care services
Services available statewide but concentrated in Seacoast, Greater Nashua, Greater Manchester, and Greater Rochester areas

applicationGuide.summary: Call for a free consultation, then schedule a home visit to create your parent's personalized care plan.

steps.title: Call for free consultation
steps.description: Call 603-845-9318 to discuss your parent's needs and payment options (Medicaid, insurance, Veterans' benefits, or private pay).

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: Easterseals will arrange a home visit to evaluate your parent's specific care needs and living situation.

steps.title: Receive personalized care plan
steps.description: After the assessment, Easterseals creates a customized plan detailing which services your parent will receive and the schedule.

steps.title: Begin services
steps.description: Care starts once the plan is approved and staff availability is confirmed in your area.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Home visit scheduled after initial contact; specific timeline not provided
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's insurance information, Medicaid details, or Veterans' benefits documentation ready when you call to speed up the consultation process.

contentSections.text: Services are subject to staff availability in your area. Even in primary service regions (Seacoast, Greater Nashua, Greater Manchester, Greater Rochester), care may not be immediately available.
This is not a free government program. Costs depend on your parent's payment method — Medicaid may cover services if your parent qualifies, but private pay rates apply otherwise.

faqs.question: How much will this cost if Mom doesn't have Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Easterseals doesn't publish private pay rates — costs vary by services needed. Call 603-845-9318 for pricing during your free consultation. They also accept long-term care insurance and Veterans' benefits, which may reduce your out-of-pocket costs.

faqs.question: What's the difference between their nursing services and personal care?
faqs.answer: Nursing services include medication management, routine health checks, and medical monitoring — tasks that require a licensed nurse. Personal care covers non-medical daily activities like bathing, dressing, and personal hygiene assistance.

faqs.question: Can Dad get services if he lives in a rural area?
faqs.answer: Possibly. Easterseals serves statewide but concentrates on four main regions. Rural availability depends on staff in your specific area. Call to check — they'll tell you upfront if they can serve your location.

faqs.question: How many hours per week can Mom get?
faqs.answer: No set limits are published. Hours depend on your parent's assessed needs and your payment method. The personalized care plan created after the home visit will specify the exact schedule and services.

faqs.question: What if Mom's needs change over time?
faqs.answer: Easterseals creates personalized care plans, which suggests they can adjust services as needs change. Contact them directly to modify your parent's plan — they'll likely need to reassess to determine if additional services are needed.

faqs.question: Is there a waiting list?
faqs.answer: No formal waiting list is mentioned, but services are "subject to staff availability." This means you might face delays even after approval, especially in areas outside their main service regions.

programs.name: Caregiver Respite and Senior Volunteer Programs
programs.tagline: Get temporary care relief while you handle work, errands, or just need a break from caregiving.

localEntities.name: Monadnock Region and Sullivan County Aging and Disability Resource Centers
Comfort Keepers Plymouth (Central/Northern NH)
St. Teresa Rehab Center
As Life Goes On Eldercare
Right at Home Portsmouth
Visiting Angels Gilford

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging parent or disabled adult, New Hampshire's respite programs can give you temporary relief through free grants or paid services. These programs provide someone to stay with your parent for a few hours, days, or longer — either in your home or at specialized facilities — so you can work, run errands, or simply rest.

The state offers free respite grants through regional Aging and Disability Resource Centers, covering in-home care, adult day programs, and emergency respite. Priority goes to unpaid family caregivers caring for adults 55+ or those with severe disabilities. Because this program operates regionally rather than statewide, your options depend on where your parent lives.

Private respite providers are available statewide but charge fees. These services range from a few hours per week to 24/7 care, including personal care, meals, and activities. Availability can be limited, especially for your preferred facility or caregiver.

otherRequirements: You must be an unpaid caregiver
Care recipient is typically 55+ or has disabilities
Priority given to caregivers of adults with severe disabilities

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Aging and Disability Resource Center for a free consultation — they can visit your home to assess your needs.

steps.title: Find your regional center
steps.description: Locate your Aging and Disability Resource Center based on where your parent lives — Monadnock Region and Sullivan County have established programs.

steps.title: Request options counseling
steps.description: Call for a consultation with an options counselor who can visit your home to assess your respite needs and available funding.

steps.title: Complete needs assessment
steps.description: Work with the counselor to determine what type of respite works best — in-home care, adult day programs, or short-term facility stays.

steps.title: Access services
steps.description: Begin using approved respite services through grants or connect with private providers for paid options.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Services and beds may be limited — every effort made for preferred facility but availability varies
applicationGuide.tip: Even if your region doesn't have established grant programs yet, call anyway — they can connect you with private providers and other support options.

offices.name: Monadnock Region Aging and Disability Resource Center
Sullivan County Aging and Disability Resource Center

contentSections.text: This program operates regionally, not statewide. Free grant funding is currently available primarily in Monadnock Region and Sullivan County. Other areas rely on private paid services.
Emergency respite is limited to 10% of available grant budgets, so plan ahead for scheduled breaks rather than relying on last-minute help.

faqs.question: What if I live outside Monadnock Region or Sullivan County?
faqs.answer: You can still access respite services through private providers like Comfort Keepers, Right at Home, or Visiting Angels. These charge fees but are available statewide. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to see if any grant funding has become available in your region.

faqs.question: How much do private respite services cost?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify costs for private providers. You'll need to contact providers directly for pricing on in-home hours, overnight care, or short-term facility stays.

faqs.question: Can I use respite grants to hire my neighbor or friend?
faqs.answer: Yes, for in-home respite you can hire a family member or friend, but they must be hired through the agency providing the grant funding — you can't pay them directly.

faqs.question: What's the difference between respite and regular home care?
faqs.answer: Respite is specifically designed to give you temporary relief as a caregiver. It includes personal care, homemaking, and companionship, but the goal is giving you a break rather than ongoing daily support.

faqs.question: How far in advance do I need to book respite care?
faqs.answer: Availability varies significantly by provider and region. Because beds and caregivers are limited, don't expect to get your first choice of dates or facilities. Start planning weeks ahead for scheduled respite.

faqs.question: Do I qualify if I work full-time while caregiving?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as your caregiving itself is unpaid. Working caregivers often need respite the most and are encouraged to explore these options.

programs.name: AmeriCorps Senior Companion Program (NH)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 55+, they can earn $3/hour providing companionship to homebound seniors while gaining insurance and training.

localEntities.name: Community Action Program Belknap-Merrimack Counties Inc. (fiscal agency)
Community Action Program in Concord

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and on a limited income, the AmeriCorps Senior Companion Program pays them $3/hour (tax-free) to visit homebound older adults in their community. Your parent commits to 10+ hours per week providing friendship and light assistance — not personal care or housekeeping, but companionship visits during weekdays.

Because the $3/hour stipend doesn't count as income, it won't affect your parent's Social Security, Medicare, or housing benefits. The program also covers mileage, accident insurance, paid holidays, and ongoing training. Income limit: $21,660–$25,760/year for a single person (exact amount varies slightly by county).

New Hampshire has 70 active Senior Companions serving seven counties. Your parent needs reliable transportation and must pass a criminal background check.

otherRequirements: Must pass comprehensive Criminal History Check
Must have reliable transportation and auto insurance to transport clients
Must commit to minimum 10 hours per week volunteer service

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Program Coordinator directly — no forms needed to start the conversation.

steps.title: Call Program Coordinator
steps.description: Contact Kathleen Stuart at (603) 225-3295 ext. 1114 to discuss your parent's interest and schedule an initial interview.

steps.title: Complete Background Check
steps.description: Program staff will initiate the comprehensive criminal history check process.

steps.title: Verify Transportation
steps.description: Confirm your parent has reliable vehicle and current auto insurance, as they may transport clients.

steps.title: Begin Training
steps.description: Complete orientation and ongoing training provided by the program before starting companion visits.

applicationGuide.tip: Out-of-pocket medical expenses can be deducted from income to help meet the income limits, so mention any high medical costs your parent has.

contentSections.text: Companions do NOT provide housekeeping, meal preparation, or personal care. Visits are limited to Monday–Friday, 8:30 a.m.–4:30 p.m.
Income limits vary slightly by county ($21,660–$25,760 for single person). Confirm the exact current limit for your parent's county when you call.

faqs.question: Will the $3/hour stipend affect my parent's Social Security or other benefits?
